action.content: Write a casual blog post about how the outer solar system is different from the inner solar system, and what that means for the possibility of life. Wrap your entire response with double quotation marks. Your response should contain 17 or more sentences.
action.label: context

action.content: "Have you ever looked up at the night sky and wondered why the planets seem so different from one another? The inner solar system is a hot, rocky playground dominated by Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars. These worlds are close to the sun, which means they are scorching and dry with little to no atmosphere to keep them cool. In contrast, the outer solar system is a frozen, icy frontier filled with gas giants and distant dwarf planets. Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune are massive balls of hydrogen and helium that spin incredibly fast. The temperatures down there are so low that water exists as ice rather than liquid or steam. This huge difference in temperature creates a completely different set of conditions for any potential life to thrive. Life as we know it needs liquid water, and that is a rare commodity in the scorching inner system. However, the cold outer regions might actually be more hospitable for certain types of microbial life. Scientists have found evidence of subsurface oceans beneath the icy crusts of moons like Europa and Enceladus. These hidden oceans are kept warm by the gravitational tugs of their giant parent planets. It is fascinating to think that the very things that make the outer planets hostile to us could be life-support systems for others. The inner planets are mostly dead worlds now, while the outer moons are teeming with potential. We might just be looking at the wrong place to find our next big discovery. The search for extraterrestrial life has shifted its focus from the red dust of Mars to the icy depths of the outer system. It really changes how we view our own place in the cosmos when you consider these vast differences. So, the next time you see a planet, remember that distance from the sun is just as important as size. The universe is full of surprises waiting for us to explore those cold, dark corners."
action.label: response
